engineering.translation: One of the Gita's most-quoted lines. svadharma — one's own dharma, the duty corresponding to one's specific role — viguṇaḥ (deficient) — beats paradharma (another's dharma) sv-anuṣṭhitāt (well-performed). Death within one's own role is preferable; the foreign duty is bhayāvahaḥ — fearful, dangerous. In engineering terms: the engineer in their actual position is more useful than the same person attempting a slot mismatched to their accumulated context. The senior backend specialist whose svadharma sits in the data-layer is more useful continuing imperfectly there than switching to frontend they will perform mediocrely. This is NOT 'follow your passion.' The verse names a precise claim about role — the accumulated context, the team's mapping of your function, the system's investment in keeping you at the post.
engineering.concrete_scenario: A senior backend engineer at a Postgres-shaped marketplace has, over six years, built deep context on this company's order-processing pipeline. She knows every edge case, every historical decision, every reason a particular index sits where it does. Now management offers her a transition to platform-frontend (React, GraphQL — newer technology, more visible, supposedly more 'modern'). She is tempted. Verse 3.35 asks her to weigh that move precisely. Her svadharma sits with backend; six years of accumulated context, the team's mapping of her function, a system invested heavily in keeping her at this specific post — everything aligns there. Frontend would not be merely 'different work.' For her, frontend constitutes paradharma in the technical sense: a job mismatched against earned context. She would deliver mediocre output through year one, draw salary as if shipping well, and create — by absence from the order pipeline — exactly the saṅkara that 3.24 names. Role-to-function mapping dissolves. The verse asks: would handling backend at 80% effectiveness produce more value than handling frontend at 100%? Krishna answers yes; his reasoning (paradharma bhayāvahaḥ) names hidden second-order costs invisible to the actor — costs surfacing later, when team drift becomes measurable, institutional memory disappears, gaps open behind departures.
engineering.falsifiability: The analog fails badly if read as 'never change roles.' The verse is not naming permanent role-fixity; it is naming the cost of role-switching against accumulated context. The engineer transitioning to a role that genuinely matches a different accumulated context (the backend engineer who has been quietly building substantial frontend context for two years and is now ready to switch) is not in the verse's bhayāvahaḥ frame. The verse's force is on the move where the new role does not match accumulated context.
engineering.counter_example: Sometimes paradharma is the right move because svadharma has become genuinely impossible — the role no longer exists, the team has restructured, the framework has been deprecated. In these cases the engineer must transition; the verse does not bind. The verse is about elective role-switching that bypasses the constraint of accumulated context.
engineering.implication: When tempted to switch roles, do the accounting honestly. List your accumulated context in the current role; list what you would be importing to the new role. If the import is small, the verse is naming the move as paradharma. The mediocre performance of the new role for the first year is not 'paying dues'; it is the cost the verse is warning about.
engineering.quotable_line: svadharma viguṇaḥ — your own role imperfect — beats paradharma well-performed. The verse names role, not passion.
